key_points: Housing survey results are not taken seriously by ResLife
Wellness housing is a hidden gem — quiet, clean, still social
Some dorms lack building-wide AC; request a unit individually
key_quotes: They really don't take what you put on the housing survey seriously.
I went Wellness my first year and it was the best. No parties on the floor is an absolute perk.
I moved into the basement of South College in 1982 — your sister and her roommate did it right.

key_points: Greek life and D1 athletics dominate social scene — by far
Non-athletes, non-Greek students still find community but must seek it out
Party scene exists but one junior says 'no one throws parties on campus'
key_quotes: The two main forms of social life at Laf are Greek life and athletics.
I feel like there's nothing to do/go to. Do you have any good recs! I have a fake also.
Greek dominated when I went there 25 years ago. That's probably the case at many small liberal arts colleges with a Greek system.

key_points: Dining changed providers 2-3 years ago — quality is now inconsistent
Upper Farinon and Marquis are nearly identical; Gilbert's is now basically a Starbucks
Complaints center on mediocrity ('mud'), not outright grossness
key_quotes: Complaints are more that it's mud rather than like outright gross or anything like that.
Gilbert's is completely different now. It's essentially a Starbucks. If you want greasy late-night grub I would suggest Lower Farinon.
I actually liked it with the other provider. It just depends on the provider.

key_points: No direct mental health service complaints found in data
Deferral and waitlist anxiety is intense — emotional posts are common
Small campus tight-knit culture may help, but isolation without a car is real
key_quotes: It's a little isolated without a car. I stayed on campus basically all the time.
I got deferred. I don't know what to feel since I would rather a straight acceptance or rejection.
My confidence has gone down a lot, I don't know what schools to apply to now.

key_points: Lafayette meets full demonstrated need but need-aware for internationals — a critical distinction
Appeals consistently work — multiple students report getting more aid after asking
Sticker price hits $70K+; many domestic students pay far less after aid
key_quotes: Accepted! Not going tho probably cause 81k a yr is gonna leave my family homeless.
I was WAITLISTED and got around 60% of my tuition covered.
We filed an appeal for more merit aid and we filed an appeal for more financial aid. We ended up getting additional aid.

key_points: Small class sizes and accessible professors are consistently praised
Engineering is a standout program with strong real-world curriculum
Liberal arts breadth requirements surprise engineering students — 1 in 4 classes non-major
key_quotes: He likes how small the classes are and how easy it is to develop relationships with professors.
Lafayette mechanical engineering program is extremely well respected — Lafayette is a top engineering school.
In the world of AI, having an engineer who is well rounded is a tremendous advantage.

key_points: Admissions office vague on decision dates — sends non-committal emails
Financial aid office is responsive and will meet with students
Career office is excellent but underused — students must proactively engage
key_quotes: Thanks for saying nothing. I thought decisions were out, but no.
The career office is not required during your time there until senior year — you need to go to that office yourself.
They replied that there is no confirmed date. You will receive email when update is posted in your application portal.

key_points: Easton is improving but campus feels isolated without a car
NYC and Philadelphia are reachable but require planning — no easy transit
Beautiful campus architecture; fraternity houses are landmark-sized buildings
key_quotes: I didn't have a car or a close friend with a car so I stayed on campus basically all the time.
Proximity to Philadelphia and NYC was fantastic.
That fraternity house looks so well built, and massive, as to be one of the main buildings of the College itself.

key_points: Career center ranked top 10 nationally — but students must use it proactively
Strong alumni network especially in finance and Northeast corridor
Name recognition drops sharply outside the Northeast — a real limitation
key_quotes: Lafayette has an incredible career office and alumni network. The career office is not required during your time there — you need to go to that office yourself.
Name recognition outside of the northeast corridor is limited.
People in finance have certainly heard of it given the proximity to Wall Street.

key_points: Full sticker price of $70K+ is genuinely hard to justify for many families
With merit or need-based aid, cost often becomes competitive or even cheap
Engineering ROI is specifically cited as strong; liberal arts ROI less clear
key_quotes: Lafayette is so expensive. For me it costs 70k and I have already appealed my merit aid once.
Getting to graduate debt free would be a massive opportunity.
Engineering is one of the best degrees for ROI at Lafayette. It's one of the school's strongest departments.

key_points: Tight-knit, friendly campus with real professor accessibility
Skews wealthy, white, and Northeast — socioeconomic diversity is a known gap
Students who engage actively love it; passive students may feel it's a bubble
key_quotes: School sometimes felt like a country club filled with rich kids.
25% of students came from the top 1% of income earners.
There are an awful lot of people from NJ, followed by PA, NY, MA/CT, and the rest of the Northeast.

key_points: **Socioeconomic diversity**: 25% of students from top 1%; campus feels like a country club
**Financial aid (international)**: Need-aware admissions means low EFC can hurt acceptance odds
**Isolation**: No car means being stuck on campus; public transit options are very limited
key_quotes: Lafayette is need aware, meaning they factor how much aid you need into their admissions decision. Needing 100% aid is a completely different ballgame.
25% of the students came from the top 1% of income earners.
I didn't have a car or a close friend with a car so I stayed on campus basically all the time. That makes it a little claustrophobic.

key_points: Outdoors club (LOSt) and motorsports shop are active, welcoming communities
Research opportunities available even as a first-year in STEM departments
Appealing financial aid actually works — many students successfully get more money
key_quotes: Stop by the motorsports shop with your kid! There'll be people to show you around.
Even as a first year you can be involved in research. Only issue is you might run into a bad professor or so.
My son is a first year student. We filed an appeal for more merit aid and we filed an appeal for more financial aid. We ended up getting additional aid.
